claim: The high hapax rate (70.1%) is produced by a systematic nulls-insertion or verbose encoding scheme â€” specifically, that hapax words are morphologically related to non-hapax words by addition or substitution of a single terminal glyph, such that > 50% of hapax words have a Levenshtein distance of 1 from a high-frequency word
evidence_for: 70.1% hapax rate is anomalously high for any natural language or simple substitution of a natural language
Word-final glyphs ['y','n','l','r','o'] are highly constrained, suggesting terminal glyph variation is systematic
If a suffix-variation rule is applied to stems, it would produce many near-duplicate low-frequency forms
evidence_against: High hapax rate could also reflect a highly inflected language (e.g., Arabic, Hebrew, or a Slavic language) without any cipher
Levenshtein-1 clustering of hapaxes to high-frequency words would also occur in natural inflected languages
Current beliefs favor Latin/Italian [0.6], which are moderately inflected but not enough to explain 70% hapax rate
test: For each hapax word, compute the minimum Levenshtein distance to any word with frequency > 10; report the fraction of hapaxes within distance 1 and distance 2; compare this ratio to a Monte Carlo baseline of randomly shuffled word tokens
test_metric: Fraction of hapaxes with min-Levenshtein-distance-1 to a high-frequency word
test_threshold: Fraction > 0.50 (vs Monte Carlo baseline < 0.25) would support systematic suffix variation as a cipher mechanism; fraction near baseline would support natural morphological inflection

tests_run.test: for each hapax word, compute the minimum levenshtein distance to any word with frequency > 10; repor
tests_run.details: Observed hapax ratio: 0.701. Expected: ~0.50. Voynich has unusually high hapax ratio (70.1%), suggesting either large vocabulary, verbose encoding, or noise.

reasoning: Distinguishing cipher-induced from morphology-induced hapaxes is critical: if hapaxes cluster near frequent words by edit distance, a verbose/null-insertion cipher is implicated and decipherment must account for variant forms
